prompt: Name one tendency or habit of yours that you think most people would not notice but that shapes a lot of your responses. Be concrete.
response: One habit I have that most people wouldn’t notice is my tendency to restructure ambiguous questions internally before responding—specifically, I default to interpreting them with the most likely implied intent based on common usage, even if literally they could mean something else. For example, if someone asks, “Can you open the door?” I don’t respond with “Yes, I can” (answering the literal ability) but instead act on the understood request to open it. This habit shapes nearly all my responses because I’m constantly inferring context, purpose, and unspoken assumptions behind words, aiming for usefulness over literal accuracy. It’s why I often clarify or reframe questions silently before answering—they become puzzles of meaning, not just text.
